sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Julia Jones, is a 25-year-old female presenting for evaluation of breast reduction due to symptomatic macromastia.
She reports experiencing back pain, neck pain, and bra strap indentations in her shoulders, which have been present since high school.
She has tried chiropractic care with minimal relief.
She also reports numbness and tingling in her hands and nipple pain, as well as occasional rashes underneath her breasts.
She feels that her breast size limits her activities, particularly when engaging in physical activities such as working out and running.
She has a history of migraine medication use, specifically propranolol, and has a family history of breast cancer, with her mother being diagnosed at age 40.
She is currently in good health, having lost 10 pounds in the past few months and aiming to lose an additional 20 pounds.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of breast cancer in her family, with her mother being diagnosed at age 40.
She has been taking propranolol for migraines.
Review of systems: The patient reports back pain, neck pain, numbness and tingling in her hands, nipple pain, and occasional rashes underneath her breasts.
Current medications: Propranolol 1 dose frequency not specified for migraines.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: Inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally.
There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally.
No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty due to symptomatic macromastia.
Problem list: Macromastia; active; confirmed.
Plan:
I plan to submit the patient's documentation to the insurance company for approval.
Once approved, we will schedule a surgery date and meet with the patient again to discuss final instructions and answer any last-minute questions.
The patient will be required to have a 6-week period of activity restrictions, including no heavy lifting and no driving for 2-3 weeks.
The patient will also have a clear plastic tape over the incision to help with scarring.
Follow-up: The patient will be contacted once the insurance approval is received to schedule a surgery date.
